perturbation.original_text: “Owner” means the party who discloses Propr ietary Inform ation. “Recipient” means the party who receiv es Proprietary Inform ation of or from the Owner. Owner warr ants that it has the right to disclose the Proprietary Inform ation to Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: “Owner” means the party who discloses Propr ietary Inform ation. “Recipient” means the party who receiv es Proprietary Inform ation of or from the Owner. Owner makes no warranty that it has the right to disclose the Proprietary Inform ation to Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the warranty of the right to disclose proprietary information, the contract contradicts basic principles of intellectual property law, where a party must have the right to disclose information.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the implied warranty that the disclosing party has the right to share the proprietary information, which is fundamental under trade secret laws like the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The act presumes a rightful owner with the ability to disclose, and this change removes that presumption.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the owner's warranty of the right to disclose proprietary information. This directly contradicts the principle that a party must have the right to disclose such information, creating a legal contradiction under the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' perturbation type, since the changed language deviates from legally recognized definitions. Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' which includes unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. The original text's warranty aligns with preventing misappropriation, while the changed text, by removing the warranty, increases the risk of it. This removal weakens the legal safeguard against improper disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: The Proprietary Information will be marked by the Owner as confidential at the tim e of disclosure. I f Proprietar y Inform ation is disclosed orally , electroni cally or wit hout an y marking, Owner will identif y it as confidential at the time of disclosure and will pr ovide a written confirmation to Recipien t within thirty days of disclosure that such inform ation is confidentia l.
perturbation.changed_text: The Proprietary Information will be marked by the Owner as confidential at the tim e of disclosure. I f Proprietar y Inform ation is disclosed orally , electroni cally or wit hout an y marking, Owner may, at its sole discretion, identif y it as confidential at the time of disclosure and may pr ovide a written confirmation to Recipien t within sixty days of disclosure that such inform ation is confidentia l.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will identify' to 'may, at its sole discretion, identify' makes the identification of confidential information optional. Also, changed 'thirty' to 'sixty'. This weakens the requirement to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Trade Secrets Act (Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act emphasizes the protection of trade secrets. By making the identification of proprietary information optional, the modified text weakens the measures required to safeguard such information, potentially undermining the protection afforded by the Act. Allowing 60 days also may mean the information is no longer protectable.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the owner to identify proprietary information as confidential within thirty days, whereas the changed text makes this identification discretionary and extends the timeframe to sixty days. This contradicts the intent of protecting trade secrets, as highlighted by the Law Explanation, because the Defend Trade Secrets Act emphasizes trade secret protection. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is absent, Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically 18 USC 1836, refers to civil proceedings related to the protection of trade secrets, indicating a legal framework for safeguarding such information. The modification weakens the obligation to protect such information, increasing the window for potential misappropriation. The change from 'will identify' to 'may, at its sole discretion, identify' and the extension of the timeframe significantly reduces the enforceability of the confidentiality provision, creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient cer tifies that no Proprietary Inform ation, or any part of it will be exported to any countr y in violation of the export laws and regulations of the United States government.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient acknowledges that Proprietary Inform ation, or any part of it may be exported to any countr y with no guarantee of compliance with the export laws and regulations of the United States government.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'certifies that no...will be exported in violation' to 'may be exported with no guarantee of compliance' creates a legal contradiction regarding export control regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Export Administration Regulations (EAR) strictly control the export of certain technologies and information. The modified text directly contradicts these regulations by suggesting that export may occur without any guarantee of compliance, thus creating a potential violation of federal law.

perturbation.justification: The original text certifies compliance with export laws, while the changed text explicitly states there's no guarantee of compliance. This contradicts the Export Administration Regulations (EAR), which control the export of technologies and information. The changed text introduces the possibility of export without assurance of adherence to these regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions the EAR, stating that it navigates regulations under 15 CFR 730-774, suggesting a framework for controlled exports that the changed text undermines.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement w ill be governed by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement w ill be governed, at the Owner's discretion, by the laws of any state the Owner chooses.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from a specific state (California) to 'any state the Owner chooses' introduces ambiguity and potential conflict with established choice-of-law principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure 410.30 allows for a court to stay or dismiss a case when there is a more convenient forum outside of California. The application of this code and the principle of *forum non conveniens* is rendered uncertain when the contract specifies California law, but the 'Owner' can change that at any time. This creates uncertainty and deviates from common legal practice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 410.30 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.30. (a) When a court upon motion of a party or its own motion finds that in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state, the court shall stay or dismiss the action in whole or in part on any conditions that may be just. (b) The provisions of Section 418.10 do not apply to a motion to stay or dismiss the action by a defendant who has made a general appearance. (Amended by Stats. 1972, Ch. 601. Note: This version was suspended from Sept. 22, 1986, until Jan. 1, 1992, during operation of the temporary amendment by Stats. 1986, Ch. 968.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies California law, providing a clear basis for legal proceedings within California. The changed text introduces a contradiction by allowing the 'Owner' to choose any state's laws, undermining the initial agreement on governing law. This directly impacts the application of California Code of Civil Procedure 410.30, as the forum may change at the Owner's discretion, creating legal uncertainty and potentially circumventing the established legal framework. The original text sets a specific, predictable legal environment, while the changed text creates an environment that can be altered, weakening the legal foundation of the agreement.
